questions.question: Explain the working of SMS Telephony in Android with a neat diagram.
questions.hint: Focus on the flow from sender to recipient and the SmsManager API
keyPoints: SMS (Short Message Service) definition - text messaging service up to 160 characters
Message composition by sender using device
Message submission to mobile network operator
Message routing through SMSC (Short Message Service Center)
Message delivery to recipient's device
Android uses SmsManager class for programmatic SMS handling

questions.question: List and explain the different types of Location Providers available in Android.
questions.hint: Remember the three main providers: GPS, Network, and Passive
keyPoints: GPS_PROVIDER - Uses satellites, most accurate, works outdoors
NETWORK_PROVIDER - Uses cell towers and Wi-Fi, works indoors
PASSIVE_PROVIDER - Receives location updates from other apps
FUSED_PROVIDER - Combines multiple sources for optimal results
Comparison of accuracy and power consumption

questions.question: What are the required permissions to implement SMS and Location services in Android?
questions.hint: Note that permissions vary based on Android version (runtime permissions for Android 6.0+)
keyPoints: SEND_SMS - For sending SMS messages
READ_SMS - For reading inbox messages
RECEIVE_SMS - For receiving SMS broadcasts
ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION - For GPS location access
ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION - For network-based location
CALL_PHONE - For making phone calls programmatically

questions.question: Explain the role of TelephonyManager in Android application development.
questions.hint: TelephonyManager is a system service accessed via getSystemService()
keyPoints: TelephonyManager provides access to phone state information
Get device ID (IMEI) for unique identification
Get network operator information (name, country)
Get call state (idle, ringing, offhook)
Get SIM card information (SIM serial, operator)
Used for monitoring incoming calls and network type

questions.question: Describe the Geocoding concept in Android Location Services.
questions.hint: Geocoding bridges human-readable addresses and GPS coordinates
keyPoints: Geocoding converts addresses to coordinates (latitude/longitude)
Reverse Geocoding converts coordinates to readable address
Geocoder class performs these conversions
Uses backend service (Google Maps API) for conversion
getFromLocation() for reverse geocoding
getFromLocationName() for forward geocoding

questions.question: What are the advantages of using SQLite database in Android mobile applications?
questions.hint: SQLite is the default database for Android - compare with SharedPreferences
keyPoints: Lightweight and embedded - no separate server required
SQL-based - supports SQL queries for complex operations
Transactional support - ACID properties ensure data integrity
Low memory footprint - suitable for mobile devices
Cross-platform compatibility
Supports multiple tables with relationships

questions.question: Design an Android application that sends an SMS notification when the device enters a specific geographical region (Geofencing). Explain the implementation with code snippets.
questions.hint: Combine Geofencing API with SmsManager for this use case
keyPoints: Use Geofence API for geographic boundary monitoring
Create Geofence with Latitude, Longitude, and radius
Use GeofencingClient to add and manage geofences
Implement GeofenceBroadcastReceiver for transition handling
Use SmsManager to send SMS on geofence transition
Request location permissions and foreground service for reliability
Handle battery optimization for continuous monitoring

questions.question: Explain the working of LocationListener in Android with a complete example of tracking user location.
questions.hint: LocationListener has four main callback methods to implement
keyPoints: LocationListener interface with onLocationChanged() method
Register listener with LocationManager.requestLocationUpdates()
Specify provider (GPS or Network) and update interval
Implement onProviderEnabled() and onProviderDisabled() callbacks
Get last known location using LocationManager.getLastKnownLocation()
Handle permissions and check if location is enabled
Unregister listener in onPause() to save battery

questions.question: Compare SMS Telephony and Location Based Services (LBS) in terms of their architecture, APIs used, and real-world applications.
questions.hint: Create a comparison table covering architecture, APIs, and use cases
keyPoints: SMS uses SmsManager API while LBS uses LocationManager/LocationClient API
SMS operates through SMSC network infrastructure
LBS uses GPS satellites or network towers for location
SMS is text-based communication (160 chars)
LBS provides geographical context (lat/long coordinates)
SMS applications: OTPs, alerts, marketing
LBS applications: navigation, delivery tracking, geofencing
Both require appropriate AndroidManifest permissions

questions.question: Write a complete Android code snippet to send an SMS message programmatically and handle the delivery report.
questions.hint: Remember to handle both sent and delivery status separately
keyPoints: Get default instance of SmsManager using SmsManager.getDefault()
Divide message if it exceeds 160 characters using divideMessage()
Use sendTextMessage() with destination address and sc address
Add PendingIntent for delivery report using DELIVERED action
Handle exceptions using try-catch blocks
BroadcastReceiver for handling delivery status
Check result code in BroadcastReceiver (RESULT_OK or error codes)

questions.question: Explain the SQLite database transactions with reference to Android implementation. How do transactions ensure data integrity?
questions.hint: SQLite transactions prevent partial updates in case of failures
keyPoints: Transaction - atomic unit of work with ACID properties
beginTransaction() to start a transaction
setTransactionSuccessful() marks successful completion
endTransaction() closes the transaction block
Auto-rollback if successful() not called before end
Ensures all operations succeed or all fail together
Example: Transfer money between accounts (debit and credit)
Performance benefit: batch operations reduce I/O

questions.question: Calculate the number of SMS segments required to send a message containing 400 characters. Also, explain how Android handles multi-part SMS messages.
questions.hint: Remember: 160 chars for single SMS, 153 chars for multi-part
keyPoints: Single SMS can carry 160 characters (7-bit encoding)
Multi-part SMS carries 153 characters per segment
Number of segments = ceil(400 / 153) = ceil(2.61) = 3 segments
Android's SmsManager.divideMessage() automatically splits message
SmsManager.sendMultipartTextMessage() sends each part
Concatenated SMS (CSMS) reassembles parts at receiver end
User Data Header (UDH) contains segment information

questions.question: Calculate the distance between two coordinates (Latitude1=12.9716, Longitude1=77.5946) and (Latitude2=13.0827, Longitude2=80.2724) using the Haversine formula.
questions.hint: Apply Haversine formula step by step with proper unit conversions
keyPoints: Haversine formula calculates great-circle distance
R = 6371 km (Earth's radius)
Convert degrees to radians: φ1=12.9716°, φ2=13.0827°
a = sin²(Δφ/2) + cos(φ1) × cos(φ2) × sin²(Δλ/2)
Distance d = 2R × arcsin(√a)
Approximately 355 km between Bangalore and Chennai

questions.question: Design a database schema for storing SMS messages and their delivery status in SQLite. Write the SQL commands to create tables and perform a transaction to insert messages atomically.
questions.hint: Use foreign key to link delivery reports to messages
keyPoints: Table 1: messages (id, phone_number, message_text, timestamp, status)
Table 2: delivery_reports (id, message_id, delivery_time, error_code)
CREATE TABLE statements with PRIMARY KEY and FOREIGN KEY
Transaction syntax: BEGIN TRANSACTION
INSERT INTO messages VALUES (...)
INSERT INTO delivery_reports VALUES (...)
COMMIT if both succeed, ROLLBACK on error
SQLiteDatabase methods: beginTransaction(), setTransactionSuccessful(), endTransaction()

questions.question: Explain the Fused Location Provider in Android and its advantages over individual location providers.
questions.hint: Fused Location Provider is part of Google Play Services, not Android framework
keyPoints: Fused Location Provider combines GPS, Network, and Passive providers
Part of Google Play Services Location API
Automatically selects best provider based on accuracy and power
Uses Kalman filter for more accurate results
Reduces battery consumption via intelligent provider switching
Works seamlessly across different Android versions
LocationRequest object specifies update criteria
